data.title: Netflix: Q3 Shows No One Can Stop This Streaming Giant
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Netflix (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:NFLX) in July 2024, I analysed the company’s second quarter earnings report and highlighted how the company’s hybrid sports strategy should allow it to stay ahead of its competitors. I had a BUY rating on the stock.

Since my article was published, the stock has jumped 17.8%, handily outperforming the S&P 500, which only gained 5.2% during the same period.

In this article, I analyse the company’s Q3 earnings report and dissect the major developments, especially the progress of its ads business.

Third Quarter Highlights

Netflix had an outstanding quarter in my opinion with the streaming giant beating on top- and bottom-line estimates once again. Q3 revenues came in at $9.82 billion, up 15% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $57.65 million. Diluted EPS of $5.40 was up 44.8% y/y and comfortably beat estimates by $0.27. While global streaming paid memberships grew 14.4% y/y for the quarter, net subscriber additions saw a considerable fall, down 42% y/y. The third quarter of last year was when the company’s password-sharing crackdown took shape, which explains the y/y decline. However, the company’s focus on profitability was clear, as operating margins came in at 29.6%, much stronger than the 22.4% figure it attained during the same period last year. Operating margins were also ahead of the company’s own guidance of 28.1%. Free cash flows also showed considerable growth, coming in at $2.19 billion, an impressive y/y growth of 15.9%.

The company once again delivered strong guidance. Q4 revenues are projected to come in at $10.13 billion, a y/y growth of 14.7%. Operating margins for the fourth quarter are expected to come in at 21.6%, which would be a significant jump from the 16.9% it generated in Q4 of last year. Diluted EPS is expected to come in at $4.23, which would translate to a massive y/y growth of 100.5%. The company also expects paid net additions to be higher in the fourth quarter, on a sequential basis, due to “normal seasonality and a strong content slate.” If these Q4 estimates come true, then it would imply that FY24 revenues would grow 15% y/y (high-end of company’s full-year guidance) and the higher-than-expected revenues have led the management to project FY24 operating margins of 27% (vs. previous guidance of 26%). Finally, management expects FY25 revenues to come in between $43 and $44 billion (y/y growth of 11-13%) and FY25 operating margins to be at 28%.

Netflix’s Content Slate Demonstrates How It Has Won the Streaming Wars

The major highlight of the company’s third quarter, to me, was how the company’s content slate has managed to not only drive subscriber growth, especially in the ads-tier, but also enabled the company to deliver strong subscriber engagement. Subscriber engagement, a metric that NFLX uses to demonstrate customer retention, for Q3, came in at about two hours per member per day. This could be attributed to the company's strong content slate, which included the likes of Perfect Couple, Monsters: The Lyle and Erik Menendez Story, and the latest seasons of Emily in Paris and Cobra Kai. It not only allowed the company to deliver strong revenue and margin growth, but also enabled it to build a strong membership base for its ads business, as evidenced by the 35% q/q jump in ads membership. The jump in ads membership has been so strong that it has exceeded the company’s ability to monetize its ad inventory.

Looking ahead, the company, in the fourth quarter, is set to launch one of the strongest content slates it has ever released in recent times in my opinion. This includes the likes of Season 2 of Squid Games, Season 4 of Outer Banks, and Season 7 of Love is Blind. At the same time, the company is also set to launch two of its most ambitious shows from Latin America:a biopic on legendary F1 driver Ayrton Senna, and 100 Years of Solitude, a show based on the iconic novel of the same name by legendary author Gabriel Garcia Marquez. Then, there is the much talked about Mike Tyson vs Jake Paul boxing match as well as 2 massive NFL matches on Christmas day, as the company ramps up its live sports programming ahead of the streaming debut of WWE Raw, which kicks off in January 2025. Finally, 2025 will also see the return of Stranger Things and Happy Gilmore along with new shows from Shonda Rhimes and Ryan Murphy.

In my opinion, NFLX has already won the streaming wars, as evidenced by its consistent revenue and profitability growth. The company even overtook a legacy Hollywood studio in the US for the first time, in Q3, with respect to demand for original programming. All of this can be attributed to its ability to consistently deliver high-quality content, which keeps users engaged. Q3 was no different. Moreover, for Q4, the company has already given some strong guidance, as mentioned in the previous section, which once again can be attributed to its content. The strong content slate has even allowed the company to increase prices in multiple regions, with Italy and Spain being the latest countries to join the list. As Netflix is set to finish the year on a high, the latest quarter proved why no competitor can come close to it.

Ads Business Shows Promise but Uncertainty Remains

When a company’s management comes out and says that it is unable to keep up with demand, it usually means positive news. While the same can be said about NFLX’s ads business, the uncertainty surrounding this business remains, which should leave investors uneasy, especially since I see its ads business as the next big catalyst for its growth. The positive takeaway, with respect to the ads business, during the third quarter, was the tremendous progress made by the company in growing its ads-tier membership. For the third quarter, as mentioned earlier, membership for its ads-tier grew 35% q/q, building on the 34% sequential growth seen in the second quarter. The ads-tier accounted for over 50% of sign-ups in countries where the company has introduced this plan. In terms of engagement, the company found that members in this tier were engaging with similar content as the non-ads plan members. Both sets also had similar views. All of these highlights are positive and banishes the questions surrounding cannibalisation of the non-ads plan and whether the ads plan would negatively impact both margins and overall profitability.

Having said that, there are still question marks surrounding the company’s ability to monetize its growing ads inventory. For a while now, management has mentioned that they are in the early stages of the ads business and that a lot of work remains to be done. Q3 was no different as the management, during the earnings call, reiterated this message. The company remains on track to launch its own ad server in Canada during Q4 before expanding it to the rest of the ads market in 2025. However, management reiterated during the earnings call that the ads business will not be making any meaningful contribution to its top- and bottom-lines in FY25.

The last time I wrote about NFLX, I did mention that investors shouldn’t judge its ads business harshly as it is making progress with each passing quarter. While I maintain that stance, I would have still preferred to see some more commentary on the progress made in this space. Instead, the message was largely the same as Q2. While there is no reason to panic given that the company’s content slate is too strong that it should help the company to grow even in FY25, the uncertainty surrounding monetization of its ads business is an area that I can’t ignore. If the same message is maintained in the fourth quarter as well, then it would be extremely concerning.

Valuation

Forward P/E Multiple Approach

Price Target

$782.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

33.9x

Projected FY25 EPS

$23.06

Source: LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), Author’s Projections, and Company’s Q3FY24 Letter to Shareholders

As mentioned earlier, management expects FY25 revenues to come in between $43 and $44 billion. Given the company’s FY25 content slate, which includes the likes of Squid Games Season 2, Stranger Things series finale, a new season of Wednesday, a new Knives Out film as well as the arrival of WWE Raw, I expect the company to have a strong year overall. FY24 revenues are set to match the company’s high-end of guidance, which was also my estimate, and given that the content slate is even more attractive for FY25 in my opinion, I don’t see any reason why the company can’t repeat the feat again in FY25. As such, I have assumed FY25 revenues to come in at $44 billion, the high-end of the company’s guidance.

Management now expects FY25 operating margins to come in at 28%, expanding by a 100 bps from FY24. This is a reasonable estimate in my opinion, given that the company has been managing its expenses in a disciplined manner, an argument I raised in my previous article on the company as well. Therefore, I have assumed FY25 operating margins of 28% for my calculations, which translates to $12.32 billion.

I have assumed FY25 non-operating expenses for the company to be $708 million, which would be approximately the average net non-operating expenses in the last five years. I have left my effective tax rate assumption to be the same as last time, which is 13.3%. Taken together, this would result in total FY25 net income of $10.1 billion. According to the company’s latest income statement (unaudited), the weighted average shares of common stock used to calculate the diluted EPS was 437.9 million. Using this figure, FY25 diluted EPS is projected to come in at $23.06, slightly lower than my previous estimate of $24.23.

The company, according to LSEG Data, currently trades at a forward P/E of 30.8x, which is close to its historical 2-year forward P/E multiple of 29.7x. Given that the company is dominating the streaming landscape, and given the structural tailwinds such as a stronger content slate in FY25, struggling competition, and the untapped potential of its advertising business, I believe it needs to trade at its 5-year historical median multiple of 33.9x. As such, I have used this multiple for my calculations.

A forward P/E of 33.9x and a projected EPS of $23.06 results in a price target of $782, which is higher than my previous target of $734, and which suggests a 14% upside from Thursday’s close. I am maintaining my BUY rating on the stock.

Risk Factors

The main risk, in addition to the uncertainty surrounding the company’s ads business, to my bull-case, involves the potential issues surrounding live streaming, a point that I raised in my last two articles on the company. While the company has largely avoided any major issues for its live programming since the Love is Blind fiasco, the stakes are much higher for the company when it comes to the two NFL games on Christmas Day and WWE. Any issues would have a significant negative impact on both the future revenues and profitability.

Concluding Thoughts

NFLX had yet another outstanding quarter in my opinion, beating on both the top- and bottom-lines once again. Management also offered strong guidance, both for the fourth quarter, as well as for FY25.

The latest quarter perfectly demonstrated the company’s ability to keep churning out high-quality content on a regular basis. With some of its biggest hits returning with new seasons in the fourth quarter as well as in FY25, and with the arrival of both NFL and WWE in its live programming category, NFLX has all but won the streaming wars in my opinion.

If there is an area of concern, it would be the uncertainty surrounding the company’s ability to monetize the ads inventory. Despite seeing strong membership growth in its ads tier, the company, during the latest quarter, did not offer any new insights into its plans to monetize its ads business. Management merely stated that the business would take time to grow, and it would have no meaningful impact on the company’s revenues and profits in FY25.

From a valuation perspective, there is still upside from current levels. And with a strong grade of A- on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum scale, I won’t be surprised if the stock blows past even my updated price target. This is Netflix’s world, and we are all just living in it. Q3 was a testament to that.

data.title: Sagimet Soars: FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation And Lancet Review Validate Denifanstat
data.description:
Sagimet Biosciences Inc. (NASDAQ:SGMT) has significantly improved recently. I believe it’s now well-positioned as a potential leader in innovative therapies for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis [MASH]. Moreover, SGMT might also soon unlock additional applications in dermatology and oncology. Since my Strong Buy rating in July 2024, based on the successful results of Phase 2 for Denifanstat, SGMT has continued advancing its clinical profile. Denifanstat is a fatty acid synthase [FASN] inhibitor that directly addresses lipid accumulation in the liver, effectively reducing fibrosis in MASH patients. Because of this, the FDA gave Denifanstat a Breakthrough Therapy designation in October 2024, and a peer review further validated its potential. SGMT is also working on a Denifanstat application for acne with Phase 3 interim data for recurrent glioblastoma multiforme [rGBMP] anticipated by yearend 2024. In my view, SGMT’s FASN inhibitory IP and MASH potential, coupled with a robust balance sheet, make it a “Strong Buy” even after its recent share price appreciation.

Improved Prospects for Denifanstat: Business Overview

Sagimet Biosciences is a biotechnology company that continues developing its MASH medicine, which could eventually have applications in dermatology and oncology. In July 2024, I rated SGMT as a “Strong Buy” due to its positive results from Denifanstat’s Phase 2b clinical trials, which showed impressive efficacy through its unique mechanism of action. This medicine is a fatty acid synthase [FASN] inhibitor that treats metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis [MASH], a disease with limited treatment alternatives and increasing prevalence.

I’ve previously outlined how Denifanstat’s mechanism of action differs from other therapies, like Eli Lilly's (LLY) tirzepatide. Denifanstat addresses lipid accumulation directly within the liver. In other words, Denifanstat might be a specific solution to advanced liver fibrosis or cirrhosis. Evidently, Denifanstat influences hepatic lipid metabolism, which is a direct benefit that alleviates fibrosis or cirrhosis for advanced MASH. On the other hand, Tirzepatide targets GLP-1 and GIP receptors, promoting insulin sensitivity and weight loss. LLY’s drug also gained attention for its results, which showed potential for treating obesity and diabetes. Tirzepatide may be more suitable for patients with early-stage MASH because those patients have more liver fat accumulation.

Since early-stage MASH patients’ symptomatology is also related to obesity due to its metabolic effects, the market speculated that Tirzepatide might also evolve into a potential treatment for weight loss. In my view, Tirzepatide’s potential for obesity was conflated with its effectiveness for MASH itself and overshadowed Denifanstat’s true prospects on MASH. I believe this is why the market has been slow to fully appreciate Denifanstat’s true potential. Fortunately, recent events since my last article suggest that the tide has shifted favorably for Denifanstat again.

Moreover, it’s worth highlighting that SGMT is also researching potential applications for pediatric MASH. The prevalence of this condition is estimated to be between 5% and 10% in the pediatric population with metabolic risks. Up to 20% of children with advanced fibrosis have MASH as well, and unfortunately, the drugs prescribed for adult MASH patients may not have the same efficacy or safety for them. On top of that, pediatric advanced fibrosis patients often suffer from a more aggressive disease progression. This is why there’s a particularly distressed niche within pediatric advanced fibrosis that might overlap SGMT’s potential with Denifanstat, as that IP might become a way to address those unmet medical needs and potentially reverse younger patients’ liver disease.

Additionally, SGMT has continued collaborating with Ascletis Pharma to study the potential of FASN inhibition for dermatology and oncology. You see, Denifanstat also has a potential use case for acne. Moreover, because Denifanstat addresses the root cause of sebum production in acne development, there's evidently another sizeable potential market this IP can target. Note that Denifanstat’s Phase 2 results met its primary and secondary endpoints. This drug has consistently shown a substantial reduction of inflammatory and non-inflammatory acne lesions. Its Phase 3 trial should soon complete enrollment by the end of 2024.

Furthermore, SGMT’s partnership with Ascletis Pharma is also testing Denifanstat for recurrent glioblastoma multiforme (rGBM). Unfortunately, rGBM is an aggressive brain cancer with high recurrence rates. They have a Phase 3 trial that should deliver an interim analysis of this indication by the end of 2024. You see, Bevacizumab is an anti-angiogenic drug that helps restrict cancer cells' nutrient supplies by interfering with their metabolic processes. Thus, Denifanstat, in combination with Bevacizumab, could be a promising treatment due to its complementary metabolic effects. Denifanstat reduces cancer’s dependence on fatty acid synthesis, complementing Bevacizumab's interference with nutrient supply. This combination therapy might indeed halt the growth and survival of cancer cells and help overcome their typical treatment resistance. In my view, this gives even more optionality for SGMT’s IP across other indications.

Denifanstat Updated Prospects: FDA and Peer-Review

Nevertheless, the most important updates since my July 2024 article are Denifanstat’s FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation and The Lancet Gastroenterology & Hepatology's recent peer-reviewed publication. SGMT’s FASCINATE-2 trial results were peer-reviewed in a publication in The Lancet Gastroenterology & Hepatology on October 11, 2024. Both of these essentially corroborate Denifanstat's impressive potential. But more importantly, these reputable validators go beyond SGMT’s management simply stating that they’re optimistic about Denifanstat.

Concretely, in October 2024, the FDA recognized Denifanstat’s significant clinical improvements over existing therapies. This recognition should expedite Denifanstat’s potential path to market, as there’s now a clear indication that regulators view this IP favorably. It’s worth mentioning that the FDA’s decision came after the promising outcomes of Denifanstat’s FASCINATE-2 trial. So this also suggests that SGMT’s trial design and Denifanstat’s effectiveness in MASH patients with moderate to severe fibrosis are aligned with the FDA’s requirements. In my view, this represents an inflection point for SGMT because SGMT’s stock price quickly surged by roughly 30% based on that news. I think the market is now seriously reassessing SGMT’s true potential with Denifanstat across all potential use cases.

Moreover, I believe that The Lancet’s peer-review of Denifanstat’s Phase 2b results also gave us additional insights into its true potential. You see, they used an AI approach that scored Denifanstat’s effectiveness based on SGMT’s data. Naturally, they also validated Denifanstat’s efficacy data, which met the primary and secondary endpoints linked to fibrosis reduction and MASH management. But more importantly, they provided insightful commentary on the data itself. The Lancet used its AI-Assisted Digital Pathology methodology to evaluate a “qFibrosis score” that decreased significantly by 6.1% in the Denifanstat group and increased by 4.6% in the placebo group (p=0.0142). They noted important biomarker changes in Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT), Cytokeratin-18 (M30), and LDL Cholesterol. Across all of these biomarkers, they corroborated that Denifanstat has a notable improvement compared to placebo.

Lastly, I think a key aspect of their peer review was that they also believed that Denifanstat’s safety profile was quite favorable. Most TEAEs seem mild with manageable risks compared to the meaningful potential benefits for severe liver disease patients. This is why I think SGMT’s prospects have significantly improved since my last article, and Denifanstat seems like a much more credible IP today than when I last covered SGMT.

Still Room to Appreciate: Valuation Analysis

From a valuation perspective, SGMT’s recent appreciation has increased its market cap to $174.3 million, placing it in microcap territory. As of Q2 2024, SGMT’s balance sheet held $96.0 million in cash and equivalents and $92.5 million in marketable securities. This amounts to $188.5 million in total available liquidity against no financial debt. Its book value stands at $183.3 million, indicating a P/B of 1.0. In my view, this shows that SGMT remains relatively cheap. For comparison, its sector’s median P/B is 2.5, suggesting that there’s still significant upside potential for SGMT from this angle.

Moreover, I estimate the company’s latest quarterly cash burn was just $5.6 million by adding its CFOs and Net CAPEX. This would suggest an ample cash runway of 8.4 years, which is exceedingly healthy. However, it’s key to remember that its cash burn will likely increase once it commences its upcoming Phase 3 trials. So, the true cash runway is likely much shorter than that, but I still believe they have enough resources for now.

Additionally, they recently filed a mixed-shelf offering to raise up to $300.0 million to fund Denifanstat’s upcoming Phase 3 trials. It’s worth mentioning that this filing could very well be executed through warrants and debt. Their recent investor presentation mentioned SGMT has enough cash through 2025. Hence, dilution isn’t imminent, from what I can infer. So, in total, I believe SGMT’s financials remain robust. Plus, management has enough alternatives to fund its research and operations for the foreseeable future.

Investment Caveats: Risk Analysis

Naturally, the main risk remains related to its clinical trials, particularly the upcoming Phase 3 trials for Denifanstat. Alternatively, if SGMT reports clinical trial setbacks, we could see some downsides to the shares. Likewise, if its competitors report significantly better results in Denifanstat’s MASH niches, it could also hit SGMT’s shares. However, I believe the recent FDA designation and its Phase 2b peer review de-risk SGMT’s IP to a great extent. Plus, as it stands now, I don’t see any meaningful dilution risks or a highly speculative IP.

So, at this point, I think that as long as SGMT continues providing timely updates and there are no delays in its research, then long-term investors have a compelling investment alternative here. SGMT’s investment profile has notably improved since I last covered and Denifanstat now appears well on track to start its pivotal Phase 3 trials. Thus, assuming that progress is expected, we could see a regulatory submission by 2026 or 2027. In my view, that timeline and prospects justify SGMT’s inherent biotech risks.

Good Time to Add: Conclusion

Overall, SGMT’s latest developments show a strengthened investment thesis. In my view, the company’s leading IP, Denifanstat, now looks well-positioned to progress into its Phase 3 trials for MASH. This would let the company tap into a large TAM with a well-differentiated, effective, and safe therapy if approved. I think this would provide significant upside potential to shareholders, which is why I reiterate a “Strong Buy” on SGMT. Moreover, as the company advances its clinical trials, it further validates its FASN inhibitory mechanisms. This feature suggests that Denifanstat might also be viable for other indications, particularly in oncology and dermatology. Ultimately, I think SGMT remains undervalued relative to its broader potential.

data.title: Apple: Why It Is One Of The Best Businesses In The World
data.description:
When it comes to Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) and the potential investment opportunity, many discussions revolve around the company's growth prospects, which are often closely tied to the success of the iPhone cycle. Sometimes, the topic of conversation also centers around AI, the prospects of the high-margin services business, virtual reality, growth in China and other key global markets, or the direction of margins.

Today, I want to take a different route and explain why I think that Apple is one of the best businesses in the world. Rather than the topics listed above, I will approach the analysis from the perspective of return on capital and its implications for value creation.

Apple's massive ROIC

Fundamentally, one way to assess how well a company performs is to first measure how much return it produces relative to its invested capital - what many know as ROIC. More specifically, "return" in this case can be defined as NOPAT, or after-tax net operating profit. "Invested capital" is the sum of fixed assets, net working capital (NWC), acquired intangibles, and goodwill, as defined by Wall Street Prep. Worth noting, many define invested capital simply as equity plus net debt, which could yield a figure similar to the original definition above considering the double-sided nature of accounting entries (i.e., debits and credits).

If the ratio above is higher than the cost of capital (think of it as the risk-adjusted opportunity cost), the company produces positive economic value, and investing in it might make sense.

In fiscal 2023, Apple's NOPAT reached $97.2 billion, or $6.25 per share, assuming an effective tax rate of 15%. Set that figure aside to be used in our ROIC numerator.

Regarding invested capital, below are the key figures:

  • Fixed assets at the end of the most recent fiscal year were $43.7 billion.
  • Net working capital was a negative number: -$47.5 billion. This is the case primarily due to Apple's large payables and other current liability balances (likely accrued expenses and deferred revenue) relative to small amounts of receivables and inventory.
  • Intangibles and goodwill added up to $36.2 billion at most, which is the balance left in non-current assets after subtracting deferred taxes and operating leases.

Given the figures above, Apple's ROIC in fiscal 2023 was $97.2 billion divided by $32.4 billion for a whopping 300%! Whether we set Apple's weighted average cost of capital at a mere 8% or an aggressive 15%, the conclusion is the same: the Cupertino company is highly efficient at producing massive amounts of value for its stakeholders, especially stock owners.

Money-making machine

The section above was quite heavy on numbers. But what does it all mean?

A stratospheric ROIC of 300%, as calculated above (to reemphasize, the ratio can be calculated in a few different ways and the answer may vary depending on the approach), reflects Apple's lean, highly efficient business model that allows the company to "make money out of thin air", grossly speaking.

For comparison, think of a traditional industrial company like Ford Motor Company (F). In order to produce $10.4 billion in non-GAAP operating profits last year (NOPAT of roughly $9.4 billion), the automaker had to rely on its massive invested capital of $68.1 billion, as calculated and reported by the company. The business model is capital-heavy: PP&E and leases amount to nearly one-fourth of total assets, while the company holds over $100 billion in receivables (that is, cash that customers have yet to pay Ford).

Ford's adjusted ROIC of less than 14%, while not pitiful, pales in comparison to Apple's 300%. Unlike the US automaker, the iPhone vendor needs very little capital to generate large amounts of profits.

Apple's fixed assets represent only 12% of total assets. A negative working capital balance suggests that Apple's vendors primarily finance the business: payables of $64.1 billion last year amounted to more than one-third of the company's total liabilities ex-debt. Meanwhile, Apple holds minimal levels of inventory, which frees up cash to be deployed elsewhere.

From the above, one can probably understand why Apple has been so aggressive at returning cash to its shareholders through dividends and stock repurchases. While the company produces large quantities of free cash flow (nearly $100 billion last fiscal year, about 28% of total assets), it barely needs any of it to continue to operate and produce even more cash in the future.

What does this mean for AAPL investors?

The narrative above helps to explain why Apple runs one of the most successful business models in the world. The discussion goes beyond the iPhone or AI to include lean, efficient operations and the company's uncanny ability to create value out of very little capital.

In my view, this helps to explain why Apple stock is so richly valued: a market cap of around $3.5 trillion and current-year earnings multiple of 34.8 times that few would consider conservative, given the modest expected 2025 EPS growth of 12% that points at a PEG of nearly 3.0 times. Still, while I don't consider Apple's share price a bargain, I appreciate the quality of the business enough to think that owning the stock at current levels might make sense.

A quick word on earnings

With Apple's earnings scheduled to be released on Thursday, October 31, this may be a good opportunity to zoom back in and talk about what to expect in a couple of weeks.

On average, analysts expect to see EPS of $1.55, which would suggest a mere 6% growth rate on revenues of $94.2 billion that, if achieved, would represent a modest 5% increase YOY. None of these figures are particularly impressive, but note that:

  1. A projected FX headwind of 1.5 percentage points to revenue growth, not something that usually matters much to any investment thesis, will likely be a drag across the P&L;
  2. These are the lowest quarterly growth rates expected to be achieved in the next five periods at least; and
  3. A low bar ahead of earnings is not always a bad thing, and can actually set the stock up for a post-earnings rally if the company delivers solid results and narrative for the following quarter.

When measuring Apple's performance vs. estimates, keep in mind the company's own guidance offered during the most recent earnings call. Using the midpoint of the outlook for each main line item of the P&L, and assuming that Wall Street is correct about top-line growth of 5%, below is what I would expect Apple's fiscal Q4 income statement to look like:

Having said the above, I don't particularly think that Apple's upcoming earnings present a short-term bullish or bearish opportunity. In fact, I believe that long-term investors who subscribe to the arguments presented earlier in this article are probably better off ignoring the day-to-day or week-to-week volatility and focusing on the company's business fundamentals and the stock's valuation instead.

data.title: MicroStrategy: Sum-Of-The-Parts Analysis Suggests Material Overvaluation
data.description:
Thesis

MicroStrategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) is a legacy software business which is in slow decline. Management has successfully pivoted the business into crypto through their accumulation of Bitcoin. While this has raised brand awareness, the share price momentum has been carried away, creating a grossly overvalued stock.

Company Overview

Most people on this forum are already familiar with MicroStrategy. For those that aren't, there are two segments to consider. The legacy of the business is software. MicroStrategy got started in 1989 as a business intelligence software provider. The company went public in 1998 and largely matched the stereotype of the dot com boom.

Shares rocketed from $7 in 1999 to in excess of $300, at the peak in 2000. It all came crashing down when the dot com bubble burst and MicroStrategy came under investigation by the SEC. While the stock price never recovered, the core software business continued to chug along, showing annual revenue growth from 2003 forward.

Despite this steady compounding in the business, valuation (as measured by price to sales) showed a steady compression. Falling from a dot com peak of 70x+ and finishing 2014 around 3x sales.

At this point, the software company started to take a turn for the worse. Following a decade of steady growth, revenue growth turned negative in 2015 and went on to contract for the next six years in a row. One can speculate on the cause of the change - did they fail to invest prudently in R&D to maintain relevance? Were new entrants attracted to their previous success and started to take share? Regardless the cause, this was a major turning point for the business. Steady growth became steady declines.

Faced with this new reality of a business in decline, management started to seek out new growth initiatives. First by attempting to innovate within their core domain, enterprise software, and then by entering a brand-new field.

Now we arrive at segment number two. In August 2020, MicroStrategy announced that they had purchased their first Bitcoin, specifically 21,454 for approximately $250 million. Over the coming years they would make many more purchases, sometimes with straight cash and other times through creative structures like Bitcoin backed debt offerings.

This brings us up to today. One stock, two segments (software and Bitcoin) and the question we seek to answer - "what is it all worth?"

The software business continues to be a slow decliner. In the August earnings report, they showed $111.4 million of total revenue, a decline of 7% y/y. In the same report they shared that the total Bitcoin holdings had increased to 226,500. As with any company, the narrative and the public shareholders are focused on the segment that's growing, not the segment that's shrinking.

While Bitcoin itself has been fairly stagnant of late (relatively to normal Bitcoin price velocity), shares of MicroStrategy have been able to maintain their momentum, recently reaching new all-time highs. For MicroStrategy, this feels like the story has come full circle. Once again, shares are trading north of 70x sales.

SoTP Valuation

MicroStrategy should be a pretty straightforward sum-of-the-parts story. If it was a standalone business, what is the software company worth? How many Bitcoin do they have? And what other puts and takes need adjusting (e.g., cash, debt, other balance sheet items).

Let's start with the software company. As mentioned above, recent quarter results were a little in excess of $100 million of revenue. Let's take that quarter and annualize it: that gives us $445.6 million of annual revenue.

Looking at a peer group of software companies, low growth software companies are currently being valued at 4x revenue. Not that dissimilar from the 3x revenue MicroStrategy was trading at before the pivot into Bitcoin.

$445.6 x 4.2 = $1.87 billion of value for the software company. This is a quick, back of the envelope number. In my opinion, it's probably overstating the valuation. 4.2x is labeled here as "low growth" while MicroStrategy's software business is no growth at the moment. Regardless, this gets us close enough.

Let's turn to the Bitcoin holdings. Q2 results showed 226,500. That's already stale information. As of September, that number has been revised up to 252,220. Bitcoin can be a volatile asset, by the time I write a value, it will already be wrong. For the sake of argument, I'm going to round up and use $70,000 as the Bitcoin price. That would make the Bitcoin holdings worth $17.7 billion.

Now for the puts and takes. The Q2 financials showed $67 million of cash and $3.8 billion of debt. Since announcing results, they have come back to the market twice, issuing an additional $1.875 million of convertible notes in September. $500 million of this was intended to retire existing debt, so we'll use $5.2 billion as our outstanding debt number.

Let's put the pieces together:

+$1.87 billion for the software company

+$17.7 billion for the Bitcoin

+$0.07 cash

-$5.2 billion for debt

=$14.44 net asset value

To put that number in context, the current market cap of the equity (@$201.67/share) is $40.8 billion. Investors are paying a 180% premium relative to this estimate of fair value.

Even if you agree with the puts and takes, and how to value the software business. Investors are paying a 130% premium to the simple value of the underlying Bitcoin holdings. That's a lot of faith and confidence in management.

Now, I can hear the objections already - I'm only looking at the current price of Bitcoin, I'm missing the fact that it will be worth significantly more in the future. Ok, an investor in MSTR would need to see Bitcoin increase to $174,000 to justify the current price of MicroStrategy. That's what is already built into the valuation.

To help you check that math, take the current market cap of $40.8 billion. Subtract the value of the software company, cash and add back the debt

$40.8 - $1.87 (software) - $0.07 (cash) + $5.2 (debt) = $44.1 billion remaining value. Divide that by 252,220 Bitcoin and you get a per coin implied value of $174,688.

If you think Bitcoin is going up, why buy an intermediary at an embedded premium when you can access Bitcoin directly through one of the main ETFs - the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), for example? This reminds me of the time when everyone was enamored with Grayscale (GBTC). Back in 2020, investors were willing to pay a premium to buy Bitcoin through the Grayscale Trust.

As time went along, Bitcoin became easier to access through more platforms and at lower fees. The premium completely disappeared. An investor could have been completely correct about the future direction of Bitcoin, but they underperformed in GBTC because they paid a premium to enter. The same phenomenon is unfolding in MSTR.

Second objection, MicroStrategy is different because they can accumulate more Bitcoin, it's not just a passive holding vehicle. This is true, but the vast majority of the Bitcoin they are buying is funded through debt issuance, not from operating cash flow of the business. You yourself can take out the margin or get a loan to buy Bitcoin. Using debt isn't unique or all that value accretive. Debt issuance to buy more Bitcoin makes MSTR larger, but it's not necessarily shareholder accretive.

If you look at the most recent statement of cash flows, net cash provided by operating activities amounted to $5.3 million for the 6 months ending June. Over that same time period, they purchased $2.4 billion worth of Bitcoin through the issuance of notes and shares (equity dilution). So, operating cash flow funded 0.2% of new Bitcoin purchases. While it makes for a good narrative that they can use the operating cash flow to increase their Bitcoin stake, in reality, operating cash flow is insignificant to the actual results. MSTR is offering investors levered Bitcoin exposure, with investors paying a greater than 100% premium to the price of the underlying Bitcoin. If you're looking for levered Bitcoin exposure, why not just buy a 2x ETF like BITX or BITU, at a 0% premium to the underlying Bitcoin price?

The last point I want to make is watch what insiders are doing. In theory, there should be no one better informed than company management teams. In the last 12 months, there have been 165 insider sells and no insider buys.

Michael Saylor himself, the mastermind behind the Bitcoin strategy, has started selling shares this year, dumping hundreds of thousands of shares worth hundreds of millions of dollars. Now people may choose to sell for a variety of reasons - rebalance their portfolio, tax planning, etc. What's interesting about Saylor's transactions is this is the first time he sold shares since 2012. If he were one to rebalance his portfolio or trim his winners, you would have expected to see some sales during the first share price boom in 2021. The lack of historical activity makes these sales seem more substantial.

While a lot has changed in 25 years, maybe the simple idea of avoiding stocks at large premiums remains the same. The last time MSTR traded at these valuations, investors went on to lose 97% of their money over the next year. While that sounds like an extreme forecast, it seems reasonable that shares could decline by 60-70% to get back to current fair value, assuming a constant Bitcoin price.

What could go wrong?

Well, overvalued stocks can always become more overvalued. Just think back to the peak of GameStop (GME) mania. Once a security price is unencumbered by fundamentals, the sky's the limit. If you believe in fundamental analysis, it looks like a compelling short. If you're looking at short-term momentum, it could be a very dangerous proposition.

data.title: John B. Sanfilippo & Son's Market Dip Has Given Investors An Opening
data.description:
I first wrote about John B. Sanfilippo & Son, Inc. (NASDAQ:JBSS) on June 14 of this year. In that initial thesis, I argued that "Managers are properly incentivized to pursue shareholder interests, and they have done so, increasing the firm's economic profitability, generating rising free cash flows, and improving the company's capital efficiency. However, my analysis shows that the company is slightly overvalued, missing out on a buy recommendation from me." Since then, the company has reported its 2024 10-K, and the combination of market conditions and the firm's intrinsic value has made me re-evaluate the firm and see it as an attractive investment opportunity, where, the first time around, I saw it as overvalued. The economics of the business remain the same, and the firm continues to align shareholder and management interests, and so, the dip in market value is a gift to long-term investors looking for a business whose management they can trust to earn profits at a rate greater than the market.

Deteriorating Market Value

Since I first wrote about the firm, Sanfilippo's share price has declined by 0.26%, and the total shareholder return (TSR) is just 1.85%. In that time, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) is up 7.65%, with a TSR of 7.95%. This is good news for anyone who liked the economics of the business, as I did, but felt that the business was priced a little too richly.

Sanfilippo Remains Profitable

Although the firm's GAAP net income has declined from $62.86 million in 2023 to $60.25 million in 2024, its net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) continues to be greater than reported income, being $63.73 million in 2024 compared to $65.69 million in 2023. I calculated NOPAT by reversing the impact of accounting distortions, such as the $2.5 million in reported interest expense, and $1.26 million in implied interest from the firm's operating, variable and not-yet commenced leases. Since 2020, the firm has compounded NOPAT by 3.2% a year.

Capital Efficiency Has Dipped

Since 2020, NOPAT margin has declined from 6.38% to 5.97%, and invested capital turns have also declined, from 2.65 to 2.51. In other words, the firm is earning less NOPAT for every dollar of revenue, and less revenue for every dollar of invested capital. The combination of declining NOPAT margin and capital efficiency has caused returns on invested capital (ROIC) to fall from 16.92% in 2020 to 14.99% in 2024. Nevertheless, ROIC remains at a very high level and has tended to go up and down rather than rise or fall consistently from year to year. This is not a systemic problem.

Readers can trust the firm to grow ROIC because, as I highlighted in my initial post, "Sanfilippo's Value Added Plan (SVA Plan), which is 30% to 40% of total compensation, successfully aligns management's interests with those of shareholders." Firms that align shareholder and management interests do well in the long run, and I expect Sanfilippo to be such a company.

Free Cash Flows Have Fallen

Free cash flows (FCF) have dipped to their lowest level of my analysis period, to -$4.3 million, driven by the expansion in invested capital. This has affected my view of whether the firm can grow dividends, but this is likely a short-term issue, as I do not anticipate invested capital to change so aggressively in future years. That said, it must be noted that since 2020, the firm has generated $213.6 million in FCF, while paying out $250 million in dividends. This reduces the quality of the firm's dividends. So, while dividend payments are supportable, the scope for growth is reduced. In addition, the firm's FCF yield is -0.36%, meaning that the firm's current FCF is neutrally attractive.

The Company is Very Attractively Priced

With a current stock price of $104, the company has a price-to-economic book value (EBV) of 1.07, a very attractive valuation that implies that the market believes Sanfilippo will never grow NOPAT by more than 7% from current levels, even though it has compounded revenue at 3.2% a year since 2020. As I have discussed in previous posts, I consider 1.1 and below to be a very attractive EBV level.

data.title: WD-40: Holding For Dividend Growth
data.description:
The WD-40 Company (NASDAQ:WDFC) stock is one of our longest-held positions. We have maintained a hold rating as shares are still expensive on valuations metrics, while the growth of the company is quite slow right now. But we are not sellers. We think the stock slowly continue to edge higher, and we will continue reinvesting the dividends here for compound growth. A hold has been wise, as the stock has been range bound for a few months.

WFDC stock is not flashy. It is not a high-growth name, nor a high-yield name. What we have here is a very slow-growing name, but one that delivers slow and consistent long-term returns. If there were a sizable pullback toward $225, we would suggest buying. While it is not a big income name, it is a wonderful dividend growth investment.

The company just reported Q4 earnings, and nothing we are seeing in the results changes our resolve to hold this long term. The outlook calls for growth in sales that could be double-digits, while margins will approach 55% for the coming year. We like holding on to this position. Let us discuss.

Revenue growth continues

WD-40 company consistently and slowly grows sales over time. That is undeniable. It enjoys ongoing demand, and internationally there is more room for the company to grow its geographic footprint. The company also enjoys pricing power for its products. In fiscal Q4, WDFC sales were $160 million, up 11% from last year. We were expecting sales of around $155 million, so this was actually a $5 million surprise versus our expectations. This also surpassed consensus by $6.8 million.

We were looking for a high single-digit increases in the top line, and we liked the actual print. Fiscal year 2024 was strong for WD-40, with double-digit revenue growth. In fiscal Q4, we saw growth in all regions once again. Overall, net sales by location for fiscal Q4 were 51% in the Americas, 37% in EIMEA, and 12% in the Asia-Pacific region.

Regional discussion

Sales were up in all regions and performed very well. Sales in the Americas increased 6%. The increase was due primarily to an increase in net sales of Multi-Use Products of $4.2 million or 7% compared to last year and there was sizable growth in Latin America, which saw sales up $5.9 million. U.S. sales dipped $1.7 million. Net sales of WD-40 Specialist products increased $0.6 million, or 6%.

Turning to EIMEA, sales growth continues to be strong, rising 16%, and this was a result of both price increases and promotions put into place by the company. We saw strength in France. There was also a 13% increase in Specialist products in the region, along with a 16% increase in Multi-Use products. But pricing was only part of the equation, volume increased as well. The EIMEA distributor sales were notably strong.

Asia-Pacific sales have rebounded nicely. Net sales rose 21% versus last year due to increases in the Asia distributor markets. Multi-Use product sales jumped 26% in the region, while Specialist sales were up a strong 24%.

Margin power

WD-40 Company has a long-term 55% gross margin target. In the guidance for the coming year, margins are targeted at 54-55%. This is strong. WD-40 has seen steady improvements in margin power of late. Gross margin in fiscal Q4 was 54.1% versus 51.4% a year ago. This increase is again a consequence of pricing increases, as well as management cutting costs. The work on general expenses continues to be beneficial. However, selling, general, and administrative expenses were up 27% in the quarter, while advertising spend was up 11% from last year, so the company is buying some of its own sales with these expense increases.

With higher sales than expected, and respectable margins, net income rose 1% to $16.8 million. For the fiscal year, net income was up 6%. EPS rose to $1.23 from $1.21 a year ago, this was actually below our expectation for $1.30 in earnings and an $0.08 miss against consensus. For the year, EPS hit $5.11, up from $4.83 last fiscal year.

Forward view

As we look ahead, the company is buying back shares and continues to boost its dividend. For the fiscal year, the company sees net sales growth of 6-11%. The margin outlook is for 54-55%, improving from 53.4% this past fiscal year. As for earnings, the company sees $5.20 to $5.45 in EPS for the year. That is slow and steady growth at the midpoint from this year's $5.11.

However, at $249 per share, WD-40 Company stock is nearly 47X FWD earnings. That is expensive. If WDFC shares fall to the $225 level, we think new money can consider some buying because the market has assigned a premium valuation to this stock for many years. We continue to hold for dividend growth, share repurchases, and long-term capital appreciation.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Endava, Cross Country Healthcare, Cybin, Equinor +
data.description:
Undercovered Dozen Introduction

The 'Undercovered' Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between October 11th - October 17th.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

AST SpaceMobile: Bulls Are In Control With New Highs Possible

Josh Arnold | Strong Buy

To say that AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) has had an interesting year would be putting it mildly. The company that is seeking to be an early mover in satellite broadband phone/data services has gone from $2 to $39, and back to $25 Just since May. It's like an early-stage biotech firm in a sense, in that it has a product with great potential but is generating nothing at the moment.

The run from $2 to $39 made it a meme stock favorite, with retail traders piling in on the way up in droves. It has also drawn the attention of those who think the stock is overhyped, and as a result, short interest sits at a very elevated 25%. Something has to give, and to be honest, I have to side with the bulls on this one unless and until they're proven wrong. For reasons which I'll explain below, I'm initiating ASTS at a strong buy rating.

One thing is for sure, irrespective of whether the next big move is up or down; it will be volatile. For reference, the 30-day rolling volatility of ASTS shares is 98%, about eight times that of the S&P 500. It's wild out there. However, that doesn't mean we have to abandon risk management principles.

Endava: Management Is Also Responsible For Value Deterioration

Carlos Bonfiglio | Hold

Endava plc (DAVA) is a UK small-cap listed on the NYSE, operating in a sector with expected growth higher than the GDP of any country, but currently dealing with a temporary slowdown and higher operating costs compared to some competitors. They are a software company focused on global digitalization. The company operates through various services, such as digital strategy consulting, process acceleration, and software engineering.

This period, which is shaking up the company's foundations, has brought to light all the potential risks that we will delve into in the thesis. However, if demand picks up again, it presents a highly profitable investment opportunity.

Given that this is an investment with high expected returns if the catalysts align, but with significant risks, my recommendation is "Hold."

Cross Country Healthcare: Strong Balance Sheet Provides Sufficient Margin Of Safety

Eli Gooch | Buy

Cross Country Healthcare, Inc. (CCRN) is a U.S. healthcare staffing company. The company makes money by helping hospitals and other healthcare employers fill positions. With a market capitalization of $416 million at the time of writing, CCRN is a small-cap stock. Over the past two decades, the stock price hasn’t done anything productive, as it is sitting at the same price as November 2002. Over the past year, it has been cut in half, down 47%. This is in line with AMN Healthcare (AMN), a similar American healthcare staffing company down 48% over the same time. However, in a direct comparison, I prefer AMN. I ultimately rate CCRN a buy based on its strong balance sheet and my long-term thesis on travel nursing.

The recent story of this business, and with healthcare staffing overall, is rapid growth resulting from the COVID-19 pandemic, followed by sharp declines as hospitals looked to find full-time employees for roles. Between 2020 and 2021, revenue more than doubled for CCRN. The top and bottom lines reached higher highs in 2022 before coming back down in 2023. As profits continue to decline into 2024, the question with CCRN and healthcare staffing in general is when they will return to growth.

Cybin: Top Value Psychedelic Stock

Jason Najum | Strong Buy

The psychedelic medicine sector provides some unique value investment opportunities. A biotech sector still in its early stages, offering substantial room for growth — yet mature enough to have reached advanced clinical trials and garnered institutional support. A speculative bet that’s no longer heavy on the speculation — with hundreds of millions in capital investment and knocking on the door of FDA approval.

This is what makes psychedelic stocks like Cybin Inc. (CYBN) a unique value proposition. The industry has reached an attractive sweet spot: advanced enough to have been considerably de-risked, yet still considered speculative enough to offer a very substantial value opportunity.

This sector isn’t attempting to just legalize or medicalize psychedelic drugs for recreational or retail use (the cannabis model) — but instead may disrupt several aspects of biotech and pharma, as well as current models of delivering mental health treatments.

Here's Why We Just Bought Another 1000 Shares Of Equinor

The Value Portfolio | Strong Buy

Equinor ASA (EQNR) is one of the largest global oil companies, with a market capitalization of roughly $70 billion. The company recently announced it was acquiring 10% of Orsted, one of the largest oil companies globally, for $2.5 billion. Ørsted is a major renewables firm and one of the largest offshore wind developers, a growing industry.

Offshore wind development is growing in popularity, costs are low but manageable, there's plenty of space available, and there's room for substantial energy production. Ørsted's backlog includes almost 8 GW of new incremental capacity over the next several years across a variety of projects, and the company is actively installing those assets.

We last discussed the company generating strong cash flow after its 2Q earnings here, and as we'll see throughout this article, a weaker share price, combined with its decisions, make the company an interesting investment.

CVR Partners: Special Situation

Assiduous Investments | Strong Buy

CVR Partners (UAN) will likely be acquired within the next six months. Currently trading at $69 per unit, it is worth $110 on a cash flow basis or $260 on a transaction comparable basis, a 60% to 375% return. Given the sleepy nature of the shareholder base, limited trading liquidity, and the MLP corporate structure, the units appear to be mispriced as the market is not properly incorporating the true value of the business or the critical domestic importance of its nitrogen fertilizer products.

Even if an acquisition is not successful, there is little downside since nitrogen fertilizer prices will continue to increase based on lack of inventory and favorable demand after the reset of the new cycle which began post summer 2023. The escalating wars in Ukraine and the Middle East will only provide further support to CVRP's intrinsic value as it threatens Europe and Asia's nitrogen fertilizer production, although this is not considered in my analysis.

Air Canada Stock: A Deep-Value Flight Ready For Takeoff

Eric Wiehe | Buy

Air Canada (AC:CA)(OTCQX:ACDVF) is Canada's largest full-service airline operating out of three key hubs in Toronto, Vancouver, and Montreal, and Air Canada serves both domestic and international routes. In the first 6 months of 2024, Air Canada served over 22 million passengers, generated more than $10.7 billion in operating revenues, and had more than 37,000 full-time employees at the end of the second quarter. As of the end of Q2 2024, Air Canada's operating fleet consists of 90 wide-body aircraft, 162 narrow-body aircraft, and 104 cargo-type planes for a total of 356 planes with the Boeing 737 MAX 8 being the most popular at 41 in their fleet.

You may have heard Air Canada in the news over the past few months which was likely in regards to a labor dispute between Air Canada and the pilots. The pilots were seeking significant wage increases and improvements in their work-life balance. After over a year of negotiations, a deal was reached for a cumulative wage increase of 42% in September 2024 retrospective to September 2023 which helped Air Canada avoid hundreds of disrupted flights.

The Other Five Fit For Mention

Willis Lease Finance's Liquidation Value Is >$200

Chris DeMuth Jr. | Strong Buy

Adjusting for the market value of Willis Lease Finance Corporation’s (WLFC) engine portfolio, their liquidation value is over $200 per share. It is a steal under that price. Comparable companies imply that it could be worth far more than that. Last quarter, I raised the question Is Willis Lease Finance Underperforming? Since then, it has started to close the gap.

Why Huntington Bancshares Has Tremendous Total Return Potential

The Dividend Bro | Buy

Huntington Bancshares Incorporated (HBAN) has rallied 52% over the past year, recovering from the regional bank crisis, with potential upside remaining. HBAN's fundamentals are strong, with sequential and year-over-year growth in loans and deposits. The dividend yield is 4%, which is above most peers in the financial sector. HBAN is trading at a premium multiple, but offers high single-digit to low double-digit return potential at a minimum.

Capital Allocation Decisions Are A Huge Tailwind At Vermilion Energy

Tamas Sandor | Buy

Vermilion Energy Inc. (VET)(VET:CA) is undervalued, trading at low EV/EBITDA and Price/Cash Flow ratios compared to sector peers, presenting a compelling buying opportunity. The company has a balanced production mix and diversified assets, with significant reserves ensuring long-term sustainable production. Management's effective capital allocation, including substantial debt reduction and a robust share buyback program, enhances shareholder returns. Despite weaker growth outlooks, Vermilion's solid portfolio and strategic positioning in oil and European gas markets support a 'BUY' recommendation.

Talen Energy: Nuclear Plants, Data Center Contract, Massive Growth

Ronald Ferrie | Buy

Talen Energy Corporation (TLN) will massively benefit from improved PJM capacity auction prices, premium data center contracts with Amazon, and rising whole sale electricity prices. The company's Susquehanna Nuclear Station has downside protection through the Inflation Reduction Act, cementing a lucrative margin. Large increases in FCF are due to changing market conditions and requires little to no capital spending on TLN's part. TLN will be a beneficiary of an underinvested market. I rate TLN as a BUY with 60% upside in 2026.

MFA Financial: MFAO Baby Bond Offering 8.3% Yield To Maturity

Jeremy LaKosh | Buy

MFA Financial, Inc. (MFA) has effectively managed interest rate volatility, maintaining stable net interest spread and margin despite higher rates, boosting net income to $65 million. (MFAO) baby bonds, with a 9% coupon, offer great returns and stability compared to common shares, making them a preferable investment. The company's debt structure, with $5 billion in long-term securitized debt, provides stability but slows earnings support from lower rates. Common shares face dividend risks due to tight distributable earnings and potential loan delinquencies, making baby bonds a safer income investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SLVO: Don't Blow This Bull Run On Covered Calls
data.description:
I'm bullish Silver (XAGUSD:CUR). From where I sit, it has a phenomenal setup, both technically and fundamentally. In a recent article for Seeking Alpha, I laid out some reasons why I'm bullish on the metal. That article is worth a read as well to understand my logic and for the technical breakout examples that I provided readers.

In this article, I will briefly summarize my fundamental bull thesis for Silver. I will highlight one of the Silver investment products that I believe people would be wise to avoid if the goal is to maximize Silver returns that track with the metal. That product is the Credit Suisse X-Links Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (NASDAQ:SLVO). First, though, let's look at the setup as I see it.

Why I Like Silver

In my opinion, Silver is a misunderstood metal. Some look at it as a monetary asset, while others look at it as more of an industrial commodity. The truth is, it is both. Yet, despite having properties that make Silver both good money and good for industry, it has lagged its sibling metals in both categories:

In the chart above, I'm showing percentages off the highs for Silver against Gold, Platinum, Palladium, and Copper since the turn of the century. Platinum and Palladium are precious metals that are used in industry, but generally not monetarily. We can see these metals clearly under-performed Silver and everything else listed. Copper, which, like Silver, has been used globally in coinage in the past, is certainly viewed primarily as an industrial metal. Dr. Copper is much closer to its 24-year price high than Silver is.

My read on this is Silver is being treated more like commodity PM peers like Platinum and Palladium than it is as a monetary asset like Gold. And there is perhaps good reason for that. Since 2022, net investment demand in Silver is projected to decline 37% from 337 million ounces to 212 million ounces in 2024:

Yet, if one views Silver primarily as an industrial metal, then the supply demand deficit of the last 6 years figures to eventually re-rate the price of the metal higher. While lower prices often help increase demand, one has to wonder how long production of an asset can lag demand growth without having any upward pressure impact on price. We'll see how this plays out. I suspect math will win again.

Annual supply has been essentially flat at 1 billion ounces per year since 2018. Over the same period, total demand for the metal is up 22% despite the large decline in net investment demand over the last two years. In my opinion, this recent decline in net investment demand is perhaps COVID-era “Silver Squeeze” investor fatigue. I think this is temporary and potentially a good opportunity to buy the metal laggard.

UBS AG ETRACS Silver Shares ETN

If the bull thesis for Silver is agreeable, the question then is: How does one put that trade on? There are certainly cases to be made for ETFs, CEFs, or even going long the producers. Those looking for income-based strategies may be swayed by the appealing yields offered by the UBS AG ETRACS Silver Shares ETN. For the sake of fund detail comparison, I'm showing SLVO against the Sprott Physical Silver Bullion Trust (PSLV):
