Yield 14.4% -
Inception 4/16/13 10/29/10
AUM $164.2m $5.6b

Source: Seeking Alpha.

The number that likely jumps off the page here is the 14.4% yield offered by SLVO. However, like many of the other call strategy funds that I've written about (examples here, here, and here), this is a highly misleading yield percentage because it is essentially just principle being returned to holders through a distribution:

The chart above shows the price of Silver, the share price of SLVO, and the monthly distributions paid to SLVO holders. While there have certainly been a few instances of large spikes in the payouts, the price of the shares themselves have fallen by over 65% over the last ten years.

When the product launched back in April 2013, SLVO was nearly $400 per share. After over 11 years and $248 in cumulative distributions per share, the current share price of SLVO is slightly under $84, as of article submission. Meaning, Day 1 buyers of SLVO have ultimately lost money. The fundamental problem with call strategies for income like the one we have with SLVO is they require active management yet do little to protect downside risk. Consider the performance of SLVO versus simply buying SLV or any other Silver-exposed instrument.

Putrid Performance

In the chart above, I'm showing SLVO against the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) — the very fund utilized in the SLVO call strategy — and two Silver-exposed funds from Sprott Inc (SII). Since the inception of SLVO, not only has the total return to SLVO holders been negative, but it is negative over a stretch of time when Silver is up more than 25%. This is a significant lack of performance.

Even during 2020, when the “Silver Squeeze” movement was happening and SLVO distributions were high compared to a typical year, the total return from the start of 2020 to the end of 2020 was still worse than SLV or PSLV. And what do SLVO holders get in return for this under-performance? They get taxed on the distributions.

Summary

It's possible to love Silver and loathe a Silver “investment product.” Such is the case for me with SLVO. In my opinion, investors should look beyond the surface of juicy yields that are apparent here and see the bigger picture.

The long-term total return for SLVO is very poor. And that poor track record comes despite price appreciation in both the metal itself and the underlying asset that is being used in the call strategy. Holders of this ETN are essentially letting the fund manager take 0.65% off the top just to give them back capital that they already had through a taxable distribution. It's a bad deal. Silver is entering what, I believe, will be a massive multi-year bull run. Don't blow Silver's big move sitting in a covered call fund that is doomed to under-perform the asset.

data.title: Corsair Gaming: May Be Worth A Second Look, A 'Buy' From Me
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

It's been a while since I made a subscriber request. This one will focus on the peripheral company Corsair Gaming, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRSR). I have covered other companies in the field, and one of my choices for investing here is actually Logitech International S.A. (LOGI). So this sector and market is no stranger to me. This is also true because I do a fair bit of gaming, and own a fair bit of Corsair peripherals and products. I can't exactly say that I have followed the company all that closely over time, but given the recent slump we've seen since early 2023, it's fair to say that the company may warrant a look here because valuations are fairly depressed, to say the least.

It's worth saying that the company entered the market "flying high". It started trading at over $35/share back in 2021 and went, for a time, up above $40/share. But since then, it's been a story of steady decline.

In this article, we'll pick apart Corsair to see what the company is made of, and what it could be worth. We're talking in this context about a company that does not pay a dividend. It also does not have an IG rating, and it has a market age of less than 5 years. This will influence my rating and my stance on Corsair, and as we move forward here we'll keep these things in mind.

So let's see what we have here.

Corsair Gaming - Some basics and fundamentals

As I said, Corsair came "flying high" during the throes of ZIRP, when spirits were high and tech companies were trading for 10 dollars on the dollar (or something close to it) despite zero profit. This company is different from the get-go because Corsair has a fair bit of profit and has never been unprofitable since being publicly traded. So starting there, I like it more.

Corsair is an American company that even at $6.75 per share has a market capitalization of over $700M. It's BB-rated, has a low debt - 24.81% long-term debt to capital, and generates earnings of between $0.18/share on the low mark, and about $1.45 during high times, with an average of about 50-60 cents per year in adjusted EPS. That would, on a 15x P/E basis, imply a share price of $7.5/share.

Corsair is a supplier of all things gaming. It has peripherals and supplies for all your gaming needs, a "full suite provider".

If you're unfamiliar with the gaming market, as long as companies don't mess up massively with one of their core products, people in this space tend to be very brand-loyal. They pick their brands and stick to them. I am a Logitech and Asus as well as an Intel/Nvidia fan. I don't go for AMD, and I mostly keep away from other brands. This is confirmed by Corsair's own data and others who look at these trends, and this is an advantage.

Corsair is different compared to Logitech in that it also sells gaming components and systems as well as peripherals. This is both good and bad. It's bad because the shelf life of components are far lower than peripherals. It's good because the company can provide a holistic/full-suite solution for needs. But even disregarding that initial argument, margins in peripherals are far better than in components - close to twice as high, in fact, on a gross margin basis.

And because there is a significant market share of growing PC gaming enthusiasts, as well as a mostly untapped market of casual gamers, we're talking about 1.7B potential customers for the company's products. Because my generation, who grew up with gaming, is getting older, the number of gaming hours has also been increasing overall.

And the sales trends for Corsair and its peripherals and components is still growing much faster than during the pre-pandemic period, with recent data from DFC insights showing a significant growth potential as well. Corsair also provides very specialized peripherals - and when new games and popular products come, the company tends to partner up to provide "flavored" experiences such as the following one.

The company is also pushing innovative new products as opposed to simply construction of computers and the like, with iCUE LINK the latest example, a technology that allows components to be assembled inside a gaming computer and reduces the wiring complexity, which is one of the things scaring people off from doing this on their own (I've built my own for 25 years).

Corsair is a company that has exposure to a refresh cycle - in this case, the refresh cycle is related to both PC components and Peripherals. When this cycle "lags", or when we're between two periods, the company's earnings and trends drop. This is exactly what has happened here, and what is expected to occur this year, with the company dropping 40% in earnings - but this is then expected to turn around as the cycle turns around. On the flip side, we can look at 2023, when earnings grew by 205% on an adjusted basis.

The lagging cycle is also impacted by the macro conditions, making consumers cautious with high-price purchases such as very expensive gaming PCs worth around $3,000. We can also expect new GPUs early next year or late this year, which are expected to drive things upward further.

But that things are looking up, that to me is indisputable.

Why?

Because the peripheral market is showing stable sales and is 60% higher than at the pre-pandemic time.

Of course, Corsair as a business is going to expect for 2025E to be a big year for upgrading PC hardware. Whether this will happen is anyone's guess, at least in 2025, but I believe it will happen at some point in 2025-2027.

Why?

Because plenty of new attractive games are coming for PC. We're talking GTA 6, Microsoft Flight Simulator, Assassin's Creed, God of War, and the new CoD (Call of Duty). All of these games will require at least some gamers to upgrade their PCs - including me. I'm already looking at a new "rig" for the first time in 5-6 years, and this will likely be priced between $3,500-$5,000 - with some components that will be Corsair. Recent launches have been very positive as well, and the company is moving more heavily into "creator" peripherals as well, which again is growing both in volume and margins.

Company revenues remain relatively choppy at this time. In order to understand the company's revenues and earnings trends, you have to understand the release cycles of peripherals and components. The last large component release was now 2 years ago. That's when revenue peaked at $281M for gaming components - since then it has moved down, to around $167M this 2Q24 as people are waiting for the new releases - which are now upcoming.

What worries me, and where I see risks, is that the company seems to be mostly unable to offset or smoothen out the peaks and valleys of its cyclicality, to a degree where even adjusted EBITDA goes negative fairly often. For 2024, the company guides for revenues of upwards of $1.35B, and at the very least $1.25 with an adjusted operating income below $100M. The company has a fair bit of cash on hand - almost $100M - but also has a net debt of $88M (fairly low for what it offers and what it generates in terms of earnings).

The key points I want to make here are these. Corsair Gaming makes peripherals and components that, based on sales numbers, are liked by their consumers. The company has moved down significantly since IPO'ing, given that its initial valuation was very inflated (in my view). However, after a 50% drop (almost) in 1 year because of poor earnings due to a cyclical development that might be set to shift in the early or next parts of 2025, there is an interesting potential opportunity here. I also want to clarify that 2QXX is always the lowest quarter.

The key question for when Corsair turns around is the refresh cycle - both for PC and peripherals, but above all for GPU. The company is expecting a GPU refresh in 6 months, which is likely to push sales. It's also important here to understand that Corsair Gaming is privy to confidential information regarding releases that the market isn't. As such, there are likely reasons for their forecasts in my view.

Let's look at what this means for the company valuation and what we can forecast.

Corsair - the upside is there, but you have to wait for it, and it might be somewhat speculative

Corsair Gaming currently trades at a conservative P/E of 18x P/E. But this is to the company's 2024E results, which are set to come in at around $0.33/share, which are significantly below both historical averages and forecasts. Now, these forecasts are not always accurate. In fact, 66.7% of the time, forecasts miss negatively, but I ascribe that to the fact that the company hasn't been on the market for a long time.

What I want to focus on are the following things.

Corsair is a good company. Corsair is unlikely to go bankrupt in the near term, or encounter difficulties, based on its low leverage, its fundamentals, and the demand for its products, which is cyclical, and this cycle is likely to see a reversal sooner rather than later. When the cycle reverses, the company is likely to see better earnings; ergo, the price/valuation is likely to respond in kind, eventually.

What sort of forecast are we talking about?

For 2025, the company is expected to revert to the tune of a 100% EPS growth, which would come to just over $0.6/share, and which would mean the P/E is around 9x. Then the company is suddenly cheap. If the cycle then continues, with new products coming in kind, as they did a few years back, the earnings growth estimate will come to 35% in 2026 as well. So this, or something like it is the upside that we can expect for the company. I would go at a 70-20 sort of expectation instead of 90-35, but I do believe that the general sentiment is correct, given the release cycles of GPUs and components.

And that is what I look at here.

Based on these release cycles, and based on Corsair's market position as well as its growth potential, I'm comfortable forecasting it not at 30 or 25, as the market would have us do, but at 15x P/E.

But even at 15x P/E, there's a fair bit of upside to be had here.

Again, what you're seeing is the upside to 15x P/E, which is inarguably conservative even if we discount these growth rates. I would then further go ahead and discount this to the aforementioned levels and go only for a price where I can get at least 20% annualized, which is the minimum level for a company without a dividend in order for me to take that risk.

That comes to $8-$9/share, and from that, I go for $8/share as a PT. That means that we're currently able to buy the company cheaply.

In terms of other analysts' targets, they tend to be more positive than I am. We're talking about a $10.43/share average PT from 7 analysts following the business with a low of $8/share and a high of $14/share. You're noticing that they tend to stick around that 15x P/E that I'm working with on the high end? I do not believe that this is a coincidence.

At this price, 4 out of 7 analysts are either at "Buy" or "Outperform", and thus I think it fair to say that the company at this point can be bought.

I say that it's now a "Buy", albeit a speculative one, and in my initiatory article on Corsair, I go with the following overall thesis.

Thesis

  • Corsair is a market-leading sort of gaming peripheral and component business with an eye towards a market upswing when the cyclical market reverts due to the release of a new generation of products, which are likely to be released, in this analyst's opinion, somewhere in 2025.
  • If this turns out to be true, the company has an upside from what I would consider to be a very low revenue and sales multiple and warrants your attention if you're somewhat risk-tolerant.
  • You need to be okay with sub-IG rating levels, you need to be okay with no dividend (for the time being), and you need to be okay with this company not having a long time in the market. If you're okay with all of this, and have a period of 3-4 years, then I see a potential upside of 20-30% per year, which meets my dividend-less RoR potential for an investment.
  • For that reason, I say "Buy" with a PT of around $8/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

I do believe the company is qualitative and well-run despite its low age, but it cannot be argued that it doesn't pay a dividend. Also, it's only cheap for the future, not for current earnings estimates. But still, it fulfills 3 out of 5 as a "Spec Buy" sort of play here.

data.title: XPeng: The Pullback Is Almost Complete - Nearing The Buy Zone
data.description:
XPEV's Mass Market Launch & Autonomous Capabilities Support The Management's FY2025 Break Even Guidance

We previously covered XPeng (NYSE:XPEV) in April 2024, discussing why we had reiterated our Buy rating despite the stock's continuous underperformance compared to its peers.

With the management opting to enter the mass market model with an extremely attractive pricing point, further aided by its expansion to the wider Asia and Southeast Asian countries, we had believed that the automaker was likely to achieve accelerating sales from H2'24 onwards, significantly aided by its low break-even price-point and compelling XNGP ADAS platform.

XPEV YTD Stock Price

Since then, XPEV has mostly traded sideways before the meteoric rise since the end of September 2024, attributed to the Chinese government's stimulus measures "aimed at supporting the economy's" recovery and boosting the domestic consumer spending.

This stimulus has already triggered the immense recovery observed in numerous Chinese ADRs, along with Chinese automotive stocks, thanks to the country's supposed recovery "out of its deflationary funk."

With regards to the automotive sector, the Chinese government has recently doubled their subsidies to 20K Yuan by August 2024, for consumers whom scrap old cars and buy new EVs capped below the price point of 300K Yuan.

This is on top of the "extended the purchase tax breaks for NEVs until 2027" at 30K yuan per passenger vehicle, allowing the typically more expensive EVs to be more affordable for Chinese customers.

As a result of the potential boost in their sales, it is unsurprising that the market has gotten exuberant surrounding the Chinese automakers' intermediate term prospects, as evidenced by their robust September 2024 deliveries and MoM/QoQ growth acceleration.

If anything, XPEV's launch of the entry-level EV, MONA M03 at between 119.8K Yuan to 155.8K Yuan by late August 2024 has been highly strategic as well, attributed to the exemplary first month delivery number at over 10K units by September 2024.

This is on top of the robust overall delivery numbers of 21.35K EVs in September 2024 (+52.1% QoQ/+39.4% YoY) and YTD deliveries of 95.86K EVs (+17.7% YoY) - with the QoQ acceleration likely attributed to the well-received mass market model.

Readers must note that EVs below the price range of 200K Yuan remains the most popular segment domestically, as opposed to XPEV's previously premium product portfolio priced mostly above 200K Yuan - underscoring why BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF) has been able to dominate the EV market with its well diversified offerings priced between 72K Yuan for BYD Seagull to 1.68M Yuan for BYD Yangwang U9 (high-performance electric supercar).

These developments are material indeed, since the mass market model allows XPEV to drive profit margin improvements after the completion of its ongoing restructuring efforts, supply chain reforms, and the highly strategic partnership with Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY).

If anything, the XPEV management already hints at "stable and maintained at mid to low-teens" overall gross margins moving forward, despite the new mass market model launch, with it building upon the 14% observed in FQ2'24 (+1.1 points QoQ/+17.9 YoY) and the vehicle margin of 6.4% (+0.9 points QoQ/+15 YoY).

While the Chinese automaker remains unprofitable on an adj net income basis at margins of -15% (+5.9 points QoQ/+40.3 YoY), the gap has been narrowing drastically, with the management's guidance of breakeven sometime in 2025 seemingly plausible.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

This is also why we believe that the consensus forward estimates appear to be rather pessimistic, since we expect FY2024/FY2025 to be trough years with FY2026 to bring forth positive bottom-lines, albeit minimal.

This is especially since the XPEV management continues to reiterate the "iPhone 4 moment for autonomous driving" by latest 2026, with the automaker already aiming to release its "AI-powered XNGP-equipped mass-production smart EVs provide a driving experience equal to that of world-class Robotics by the second half of 2025" across 95% of China's roads.

This also builds upon Tesla's (TSLA) guidance of starting "unsupervised autonomous driving in Texas and California in 2025 for the firm's Model 3 and Model Y," as the autonomous driving capability is increasingly rolled out and accepted globally.

As a result of the growing revenues from services and others (including software) at $0.18B (+28.5% QoQ/+102.5% YoY), we believe that the consensus forward estimates are likely to upgraded in the intermediate term, once XPEV continues to execute the positive gross profit margins and narrowing net margin losses over the next few quarters.

XPEV Valuations

While XPEV may appear to be trading at a premium FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.57x, compared to the previous article at 0.50x and the sector median of 1.29x, we beg to differ.

Assuming that the automaker is able to generate the FY2025 revenues of $9.1B, it appears that the resultant EV/Sales valuations of 0.94x remain reasonable enough for long-sighted investors.

This is compared to its Chinese automaker peers, including BYD at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.12x, Li Auto (LI) at 0.77x, and NIO (NIO) at 1.33x, with it apparent that XPEV remains a compelling Buy after a moderate retracement.

At the same time, with FY2026 likely to bring forth positive profit margins, we believe that XPEV remains well capitalized to weather the near-term cash burn, based on the $5.14B of cash on balance sheet as of FQ2'24 (-10.2% QoQ/+10.5% YoY) - significantly aided by the strategic partnership with VWAGY.

Lastly, readers must not forget the potential boost from its rather speculative launch of its modular flying car, XPeng Aeroht in 2026, with the market likely closely watching the upcoming showcase in November 2024.

So, Is XPEV Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

XPEV 2Y Stock Price

For now, as discussed in our BYD article here, it is apparent that the recent rally has been overly done with most Chinese ADRs already returning part of their recent gains, XPEV included.

With the stock now retesting the historical support levels of $11 after failing to break out of the $13s resistance levels, we believe that further moderation is likely as traders unlock most of their gains at these inflated levels.

Despite so, we believe that the ongoing retracement is a gift indeed, since a further pullback to its next support levels of $9s and $10s is likely to trigger a far more attractive entry point, based on the estimated FY2025 EV/Sales valuations of 0.62x - with it bringing the stock nearer to its historical valuations and its peers.

As a result of its high-growth prospects along with the intermediate term tailwinds from the automotive subsidies, we are maintaining our Buy rating for the XPEV stock once the pullback is complete.

Risk Warning

It goes without saying that XPEV is only suitable for those with a long investing trajectory, since the automaker is only expected to record break even by FY2025 and minimal profitability in FY2026.

At the same time, readers may to monitor its upcoming execution, since the management has bet its long-term growth strategy on overseas markets - with it expected to play a "more significant role in driving both sales and profit growth," building upon the fact that overseas sales accounted for more than 10% of its total sales by the latest quarter.

The aggressive global expansion may potentially trigger moderate bottom-line headwinds, with XPEV expecting to enter UK, Australia, and Southeast Asia in H2'24, building upon its growing presence in the EU, Middle East, and LatAm - attributed to the projected doubling in its international sales stores (and likely operating expenses) over the next two quarters.

At the same time, readers must not forget that numerous countries are planning, or have imposed import tariffs for Chinese made EVs - with it potentially triggering demand headwinds and top/bottom-lines misses.

Combined with its Chinese ADR status and the potential volatility arising from the ongoing US elections through November 2024, it goes without saying that XPEV is only suitable for those with moderate geopolitical risk appetite.
